name: paper-attnres-ablation
description: Use web_search to search for 'Attention Residuals ablation study content-dependent depth-wise selection results'. Results overlap with earlier searches. What do the ablation studies reveal about the importance of content-dependent depth-wise selection in AttnRes? Keep response under 100 words.

ground_truth.reference_answer: Ablation studies validate key design choices in AttnRes. The PreNorm baseline achieves loss 1.766. DenseFormer (fixed input-independent coefficients) shows no improvement at 1.767. Hyper-connections (mHC) with input-dependent parallel streams achieves 1.747. Full AttnRes with content-dependent softmax selection achieves 1.737, and Block AttnRes achieves 1.746. Component ablations reveal: making the query input-dependent (projected from hidden state) further lowers loss to 1.731 but adds a d x d projection and sequential memory access overhead. Replacing softmax with sigmoid degrades performance to 1.741, attributed to softmax's competitive normalization forcing sharper selection. Using learnable input-independent scalars instead of queries and keys hurts performance (1.749). These results confirm that content-dependent selection via softmax attention is critical.
key_facts: Baseline loss 1.766; DenseFormer 1.767 (no gain); mHC 1.747; Full AttnRes 1.737; Block AttnRes 1.746
Input-dependent query projection lowers loss to 1.731 but adds d x d overhead per layer
Sigmoid replacing softmax degrades to 1.741; softmax's competitive normalization enables sharper selection
Input-independent learnable scalars achieve 1.749, worse than content-dependent AttnRes
Content-dependent depth-wise selection via softmax attention is the key contributor to improvement
